key_points: Natural vegetation is influenced by climate, soil, relief and altitude
India has rich biodiversity with thousands of plant and animal species
Tropical evergreen forests are dense forests with multilayer vegetation found in high rainfall regions
Tropical deciduous forests are divided into moist and dry deciduous forests
Thorn forests are adapted to dry climates with plants having long roots and small leaves
Montane forests change with altitude and include coniferous forests at higher elevations
Mangrove forests protect coastal regions from erosion and storms
Wildlife habitats include forests, mountains, grasslands and wetlands
Deforestation, pollution and hunting are major threats to biodiversity
Conservation programs help protect endangered species and maintain ecological balance
examples: Example of evergreen forest trees: Ebony and Mahogany
Example of deciduous forest trees: Teak and Sal
Example of thorn forest vegetation: Cactus and Babool
Example of montane forest animals: Snow leopard and Yak
Example of mangrove forest region: Sundarbans delta
Example of endangered animal species: Tiger
formulas: Biodiversity conservation = Protection of plant and animal species
Ecosystem balance = Interaction between vegetation, wildlife and environment
Forest ecosystem = Plants + Animals + Climate + Soil
Sustainable development = Conservation of natural resources for future generations
exam_tips: Focus on the relationship between climate and vegetation distribution
Learn the classification of forests and their characteristics
Understand ecological importance of mangrove ecosystems
Remember the role of biosphere reserves in conservation
Study the impact of human activities such as deforestation on biodiversity
